marks.dropCaption: A hillside villa with a locked east wing and one inattentive night guard.
marks.dressing: Manicured grounds, iron gate, one guard on rotation. Owner at the opera until midnight.

marks.dropCaption: A sun-faded villa whose owner trusts a Rottweiler over cameras.
marks.dressing: Quiet neighbourhood, motion-sensor lights only on the south side. Dog is kennelled on Thursdays.

marks.dropCaption: A modern villa with professional security — and one predictable patrol gap.
marks.dressing: Private security firm on contract, cameras everywhere, but the rear garden blind-spot is thirty seconds wide.

marks.dropCaption: Three generations of old money, locked behind surprisingly new doors.
marks.dressing: Forty rooms, staff quarters in the west annex. The groundskeepers are off Thursdays — I made it Thursday.

marks.dropCaption: A crumbling estate with a wine cellar that holds more than bottles.
marks.dressing: Old stone walls, unreliable CCTV, one live-in caretaker who sleeps heavily.

marks.dropCaption: A country estate whose owner values art over alarm codes.
marks.dressing: Sweeping grounds, gated entry, motion sensors inside only. Owner is away for the weekend.

marks.dropCaption: Forty-two floors up — and the security is private, not police.
marks.dressing: Two merged units, private elevator key-coded, concierge on duty 24 hours. We go in through the service shaft.

marks.dropCaption: Sixty-first floor, west terrace, a vault disguised as a wine cooler.
marks.dressing: Building security is contract staff. Floor 61 is the only residential floor above 58 — foot traffic is zero at 2 AM.

marks.dropCaption: A rooftop penthouse whose owner installed a panic room but forgot about the dumbwaiter.
marks.dressing: Rooftop access via service lift, panic room on the east side. We stay west. Owner lands from Geneva at six AM.
